claim: Evaluating agent systems requires assessing both the context (tool selection, tool outputs) and the final output, not just the output in isolation
actionable: When designing evaluation suites for agents, define multiple evaluation dimensions: tool call correctness, context relevance, answer relevance, and final output quality.
source_title: Evaluating Supervisor Agents with MLflow on Databricks

claim: The same input to an LLM can produce many semantically equivalent but syntactically different outputs, making traditional ML metrics inadequate for GenAI evaluation
actionable: Use LLM-as-judge evaluation methods rather than string-matching metrics when evaluating generative AI outputs.
source_title: Evaluating Supervisor Agents with MLflow on Databricks

claim: MLflow traces capture the entire end-to-end inference lifecycle from input through all intermediate steps (spans) to final output, enabling comprehensive agentic system evaluation
actionable: Use MLflow traces as your foundation for evaluation: extract all spans (LLM calls, tool calls, intermediate outputs) and feed this complete context to your judge models.
source_title: Evaluating Supervisor Agents with MLflow on Databricks

claim: MLflow provides three types of scorers: built-in LLM judges (predefined metrics like tool_call_correctness), custom judges (user-defined rubrics), and custom scores (programmatic metrics via Python decorators)
actionable: Start with built-in judges for standard metrics, use custom judges to define domain-specific success criteria as system prompts, and use custom scores for programmatic/latency metrics.
source_title: Evaluating Supervisor Agents with MLflow on Databricks

claim: For supervisor/router agents, critical evaluation dimensions include: routing correctness (selecting the right sub-agent), output accuracy, tool call efficiency (minimal unnecessary calls), and end-to-end latency
actionable: When building a supervisor agent evaluation suite, explicitly define metrics for each of these four dimensions and construct evaluation datasets with ground truth tool expectations.
source_title: Evaluating Supervisor Agents with MLflow on Databricks

claim: You must provide ground truth evaluation datasets to LLM-as-judge systems; they cannot evaluate blind without benchmarks, making data collection a critical upfront task
actionable: Before building an evaluation suite, invest time in creating ground truth datasets with expected outputs for different input categories and edge cases.
source_title: Evaluating Supervisor Agents with MLflow on Databricks

claim: Evaluation should be a continuous process during agent development, not a one-time final validation, allowing you to benchmark each unit before the whole system
actionable: Integrate evaluation checks into your development workflow; run evaluations after each significant agent modification to catch regressions early.
source_title: Evaluating Supervisor Agents with MLflow on Databricks

claim: MLflow's built-in judge for tool_call_correctness evaluates whether the correct tool is selected; custom judges let you define rubric-based success criteria with instructions passed as system prompts
actionable: Use the make_judge API to create custom LLM judges; write detailed rubric instructions that become the system prompt for the evaluating LLM.
source_title: Evaluating Supervisor Agents with MLflow on Databricks
